title: The Warmth Episode

jokes.text: Now, that sign says "applesauce." No, no, I'm kidding. It says "applause."
jokes.setup: Larry pointing to an applause sign and misidentifying it
jokes.punchline: The immediate correction reveals he was joking
jokes.explanation: Simple misdirection joke. Clean structure but fairly predictable warmup material.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Now, that sign says "applesauce." No, no, I'm kidding. It says "applause."

jokes.text: If I seem a little confused, I'm just in a lot of turmoil. I just feel so stupid. I don't know if I should be pulling... for Bosnia or Herzegovina.
jokes.setup: Larry admits confusion about current events
jokes.punchline: He's confused about which side to support in a conflict between regions that were part of the same country
jokes.explanation: Good topical humor that reveals Larry's shallow understanding of world events. Character-appropriate confusion.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: If I seem a little confused, I'm just in a lot of turmoil. I just feel so stupid. I don't know if I should be pulling... for Bosnia or Herzegovina.

jokes.text: So, Bill Clinton intends to actually stop the fighting in Bosnia. At least, that's what he was overheard saying to some chick in a bar.
jokes.setup: Serious statement about Clinton's Bosnia policy
jokes.punchline: Undermines it by suggesting Clinton was just trying to impress a woman
jokes.explanation: Solid political humor with good misdirection structure. Plays on Clinton's reputation.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: So, Bill Clinton intends to actually stop the fighting in Bosnia. At least, that's what he was overheard saying to some chick in a bar.

jokes.text: The surgeon general announced today it is now official. You're not fully clean unless your "Zestfully" clean.
jokes.setup: Mock-serious announcement from the surgeon general
jokes.punchline: It's just a Zest soap commercial tagline presented as medical advice
jokes.explanation: Weak commercial parody. The audience silence suggests it bombed, which becomes the real joke.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: The surgeon general announced today it is now official. You're not fully clean unless your "Zestfully" clean.

jokes.text: I told you. I told you that joke would not work, didn't I, Hank?
jokes.setup: Larry's joke just bombed with the audience
jokes.punchline: He breaks character to scold Hank about it on-air
jokes.explanation: Brilliant meta-humor. Larry's insecurity and willingness to throw Hank under the bus on live TV is peak character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: I told you. I told you that joke would not work, didn't I, Hank?

jokes.text: Well, it's very funny to me.
jokes.setup: Larry just publicly blamed Hank for a bad joke
jokes.punchline: Hank's earnest, oblivious defense of the terrible joke
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank response. His complete obliviousness to Larry's passive-aggression is character gold.

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: Well, it's very funny to me.

jokes.text: The surgeon general announced it is now official. You're not clean... Maybe I didn't say it exactly right. You're not fully clean unless your "Zestfully" clean.
jokes.setup: Larry decides to try the failed joke again
jokes.punchline: He botches it even worse, stopping mid-sentence to self-correct
jokes.explanation: Escalation of the bombing joke. Larry's desperation to make it work makes it even worse. Classic cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: The surgeon general announced it is now official. You're not clean... Maybe I didn't say it exactly right. You're not fully clean unless your "Zestfully" clean.

jokes.text: It gets funnier every time I hear it. See, you can't go for the masses. No, I'll just go... Just announcers around the country are laughing.
jokes.setup: Larry's joke has now failed twice
jokes.punchline: He rationalizes that it's too sophisticated for regular people, only announcers get it
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character work. Larry's delusional self-defense mechanism is perfectly in character and hilariously specific.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: It gets funnier every time I hear it. See, you can't go for the masses. No, I'll just go... Just announcers around the country are laughing.

jokes.text: it's 11:40 on Thursday... and that can mean only one thing... it's time for a "Hank Weight Update."
jokes.setup: Larry announces a regular segment with dramatic buildup
jokes.punchline: It's just weighing Hank, treated like breaking news
jokes.explanation: Good setup for ongoing running gag. The dramatic presentation of mundane content is classic talk show satire.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: it's 11:40 on Thursday... and that can mean only one thing... it's time for a "Hank Weight Update."

jokes.text: One hundred ninety-eight and a half.
jokes.setup: Hank reveals his current weight
jokes.punchline: The audience's enthusiastic reaction to this mundane information
jokes.explanation: The humor is in the audience treating weight loss like a major achievement. Hank's earnestness sells it.

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: One hundred ninety-eight and a half.

jokes.text: This picture was taken an hour ago in the commissary.
jokes.setup: Richard shows what he claims is an old photo of fat Hank
jokes.punchline: It's actually from an hour ago, revealing Hank hasn't lost weight
jokes.explanation: Excellent visual gag with perfect timing. Completely undercuts Hank's weight loss claims with brutal efficiency.

dialogue.speaker: Richard Simmons
dialogue.line: This picture was taken an hour ago in the commissary.

jokes.text: I have not mentioned the fact that he's bald.
jokes.setup: Richard claims he's being supportive and not putting Larry down
jokes.punchline: He immediately mentions Larry's baldness while claiming he won't
jokes.explanation: Classic passive-aggressive humor. Richard's 'accidental' insult while claiming moral high ground.

dialogue.speaker: Richard Simmons
dialogue.line: I have not mentioned the fact that he's bald.

jokes.text: I don't like when you scold me.
jokes.setup: Larry has been defensive about Richard's comments
jokes.punchline: He sounds like a petulant child complaining about being scolded
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry moment. His childish vulnerability breaks through his host persona in an uncomfortable way.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: I don't like when you scold me.

jokes.text: I think Richard Simmons is very funny, but what's he always jumping around for? He's got the energy of a squirrel. I don't think that's healthy, is it?
jokes.setup: Larry compliments Richard then questions his behavior
jokes.punchline: Comparing Richard's energy to a squirrel and suggesting it's unhealthy
jokes.explanation: Good observational humor with nice animal comparison. Larry's passive-aggressive analysis is in character.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: I think Richard Simmons is very funny, but what's he always jumping around for? He's got the energy of a squirrel. I don't think that's healthy, is it?

jokes.text: I think it's all a front. I think we're gonna catch him... going through a Dumpster at Winchell's one night, and it'll all be over. Right there on the Enquirer, him eating a few doughnuts from the trash.
jokes.setup: Larry suggests Richard's fitness persona is fake
jokes.punchline: Vivid image of Richard desperately eating trash donuts, ending up on a tabloid
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with vivid, specific imagery. The Winchell's dumpster detail makes it memorably dark and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: I think it's all a front. I think we're gonna catch him... going through a Dumpster at Winchell's one night, and it'll all be over. Right there on the Enquirer, him eating a few doughnuts from the trash.

jokes.text: Every 30 days, they take their thumb out of their ass, scribble down numbers.
jokes.setup: Artie describes network research people
jokes.punchline: Crude but vivid description of their lazy, meaningless work process
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie line. Crude but economical description that captures his cynical industry veteran perspective.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Every 30 days, they take their thumb out of their ass, scribble down numbers.

jokes.text: It's not the size of the number. It's the motion of the ocean.
jokes.setup: Larry is worried about his low likability rating
jokes.punchline: Artie uses a sexual euphemism to dismiss the importance of ratings
jokes.explanation: Clichéd sexual pun but delivered in character. Artie's crude comfort attempts are true to form.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: It's not the size of the number. It's the motion of the ocean.

jokes.text: Oh, for God's sakes, Artie. I wish you were a woman. I'd feel a little better when you say that.
jokes.setup: Artie just made a sexual innuendo to comfort Larry
jokes.punchline: Larry wishes Artie were a woman so the sexual reference would be less uncomfortable
jokes.explanation: Great character moment. Larry's discomfort with male intimacy expressed through absurd logic is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Oh, for God's sakes, Artie. I wish you were a woman. I'd feel a little better when you say that.

jokes.text: Look, he's below Mengele.
jokes.setup: Larry looking at likability ratings on the research report
jokes.punchline: Rush Limbaugh is rated less likable than a Nazi war criminal
jokes.explanation: Sharp political humor with good economy. The extreme comparison makes the point efficiently.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Look, he's below Mengele.

jokes.text: Is there something we can do about my likability?
jokes.setup: Larry has been obsessing over his ratings
jokes.punchline: His desperate, pathetic question about manufacturing likability
jokes.explanation: Pure Larry insecurity. The idea that likability can be engineered reveals his fundamental disconnection from authenticity.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Is there something we can do about my likability?

jokes.text: Yeah. That's why I have someone walk me to my car at night.
jokes.setup: Artie mentions Arsenio said he'd kick their ass in Entertainment Weekly
jokes.punchline: Larry's paranoid overreaction to a figurative threat
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry paranoia. Taking a showbiz rivalry threat literally reveals his neurotic worldview beautifully.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Yeah. That's why I have someone walk me to my car at night.

jokes.text: In your own way.
jokes.setup: Larry asks his wife if he's a nice guy, she says sure
jokes.punchline: Her qualifier 'in your own way' completely undermines the reassurance
jokes.explanation: Perfectly damning with faint praise. Three words that completely destroy Larry's fishing for compliments.

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie Sanders
dialogue.line: In your own way.

jokes.text: You're a funny person, and all funny people have a certain amount of rage.
jokes.setup: Jeannie explaining Larry's hostility issue
jokes.punchline: She psychoanalyzes comedy as coming from anger, which explains Larry
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-commentary on comedy. Jeannie's clinical assessment of Larry doubles as show business insight.

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie Sanders
dialogue.line: You're a funny person, and all funny people have a certain amount of rage.

jokes.text: People watch your show because you're partly an asshole.
jokes.setup: Jeannie continuing to explain Larry's appeal
jokes.punchline: Brutally honest assessment that his unlikability is actually his selling point
jokes.explanation: Brutal and brilliant. Jeannie's blunt honesty perfectly captures Larry's paradox and the show's central premise.

dialogue.speaker: Jeannie Sanders
dialogue.line: People watch your show because you're partly an asshole.

jokes.text: For God's sakes. It's my fucking living room too, honey. There you go.
jokes.setup: Jeannie asks Larry not to eat in the living room
jokes.punchline: Larry's hostile outburst immediately proves her point about his anger
jokes.explanation: Perfect immediate callback. Larry proves Jeannie's 'rage' theory within seconds, with her 'There you go' sealing it.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: For God's sakes. It's my fucking living room too, honey. There you go.

jokes.text: I don't mean to sound like a jerk. I'm Larry Sanders.
jokes.setup: Security guard asking for ID
jokes.punchline: Larry's attempt at humility immediately followed by name-dropping himself
jokes.explanation: Perfect contradiction. Larry's attempt to be modest is immediately undermined by his expectation of celebrity recognition.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: I don't mean to sound like a jerk. I'm Larry Sanders.

jokes.text: What the fuck are you doing, man? That's Mr. Sanders!
jokes.setup: New security guard Rick didn't recognize Larry
jokes.punchline: Don's aggressive scolding reveals the toxic hierarchy
jokes.explanation: Good workplace hierarchy satire. Don's overprotective reaction shows how celebrity worship corrupts normal interactions.

dialogue.speaker: Don
dialogue.line: What the fuck are you doing, man? That's Mr. Sanders!

jokes.text: One time, Joan Embery brought something on I was attracted to. Bad policy to date a guest... especially when they have to be back at the zoo at 6:30.
jokes.setup: Larry asked about romantic involvement with guests
jokes.punchline: He was attracted to an animal that Joan Embery brought, not her
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with perfect setup and payoff. The zoo timing detail makes it even funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: One time, Joan Embery brought something on I was attracted to. Bad policy to date a guest... especially when they have to be back at the zoo at 6:30.

jokes.text: Well, how about if I sit in this chair... and you take pictures of me?
jokes.setup: Photographer suggests elaborate dart-throwing photo setup
jokes.punchline: Larry's aggressively simple counter-proposal
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry deflation. His complete resistance to creative concepts in favor of basic professionalism is very in character.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Well, how about if I sit in this chair... and you take pictures of me?

jokes.text: They fired the guard who wouldn't let you in this morning.
jokes.setup: Beverly mentions taking care of Larry's security problem
jokes.punchline: She fired the guard for doing his job properly
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the security incident. Beverly's overreaction shows how celebrity privilege corrupts normal workplace fairness.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: They fired the guard who wouldn't let you in this morning.

jokes.text: You've gotta stop letting that man pluck your nerves the way you do. Maybe you're right. "Pluck your nerves." Is that a black thing?
jokes.setup: Beverly uses the phrase 'pluck your nerves'
jokes.punchline: Larry's awkward racial question about the expression
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry awkwardness. His inappropriate curiosity about racial linguistics is cringily in character.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: You've gotta stop letting that man pluck your nerves the way you do.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Maybe you're right. "Pluck your nerves." Is that a black thing?

jokes.text: You're such an idiot. But the homosexual community likes me.
jokes.setup: Beverly calls Larry an idiot for his racial question
jokes.punchline: Larry's non-sequitur about gay community approval
jokes.explanation: Larry's desperate deflection to any positive demographic feedback. The random topic jump is perfectly neurotic.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: You're such an idiot.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: But the homosexual community likes me.

jokes.text: Pass. Let's call a brief moratorium... on all world's youngest, world's oldest pitches.
jokes.setup: Paula pitches various 'world's youngest/oldest' guest ideas
jokes.punchline: Larry's frustrated ban on this tired category of bookings
jokes.explanation: Good industry satire. Larry's exasperation with lazy booking categories is relatable and well-structured.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Pass. Let's call a brief moratorium... on all world's youngest, world's oldest pitches.

jokes.text: Look at this! We have the world's best skier under the age of four. Very funny. Pass.
jokes.setup: Larry just banned world's youngest/oldest pitches
jokes.punchline: Paula immediately pitches another one, proving she wasn't listening
jokes.explanation: Perfect immediate callback showing Paula's complete obliviousness. The absurdity of a four-year-old skier adds to it.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Look at this! We have the world's best skier under the age of four. Very funny. Pass.

jokes.text: If 20 random people decided... that you'd look better with a crew cut, would you get one? Yes.
jokes.setup: Artie questions whether Larry should listen to focus groups
jokes.punchline: Larry's immediate 'yes' reveals his total lack of self-confidence
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment. Larry's instant agreement reveals his complete willingness to abandon any personal identity for approval.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: If 20 random people decided... that you'd look better with a crew cut, would you get one?

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Yes.

jokes.text: Is this an affiliate problem? I would be more than happy... to fly out to Bumfuck, Arkansas or wherever... and give them the old dog and pony show.
jokes.setup: Hank interrupts a private conversation between Larry and Artie
jokes.punchline: His eagerness to travel anywhere for attention, even to 'Bumfuck, Arkansas'
jokes.explanation: Good Hank characterization. His desperate eagerness for any opportunity and crude geographical reference are in character.

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: Is this an affiliate problem? I would be more than happy... to fly out to Bumfuck, Arkansas or wherever... and give them the old dog and pony show.

jokes.text: I'm with Widenfield-Johnson. They are the best. They handle everything... appearances, endorsements, charities. I didn't know you did charity work. Oh, yes. Really? I give of myself. Good for you. The pay's great.
jokes.setup: Hank talks about doing charity work and giving of himself
jokes.punchline: He reveals he gets paid for charity work
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment. His complete misunderstanding of charity while maintaining self-righteousness is beautiful character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: I'm with Widenfield-Johnson. They are the best. They handle everything... appearances, endorsements, charities.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: I didn't know you did charity work.

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: Oh, yes.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: I give of myself.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Good for you.

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: The pay's great.

jokes.text: They got me, you know, TB this year. Tuberculosis? Yes, sir. The lung disease.
jokes.setup: Hank mentions his publicist got him 'TB this year'
jokes.punchline: He's the celebrity spokesperson for tuberculosis, a terrible disease
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor. The idea of TB as a celebrity endorsement opportunity is darkly hilarious and perfectly stupid.

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: They got me, you know, TB this year.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Tuberculosis?

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: Yes, sir. The lung disease.

jokes.text: Contagious?
jokes.setup: Larry mentions TB being contagious
jokes.punchline: Hank's shocked realization that his disease endorsement might be dangerous
jokes.explanation: Perfect one-word punchline. Hank's dawning horror at his publicist's terrible advice is beautifully economical.

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: Contagious?

jokes.text: Contagious?
jokes.setup: Hank repeats his shocked question about TB being contagious
jokes.punchline: He's still processing this terrible realization
jokes.explanation: Good callback repetition. Hank's continued shock extends the joke nicely without overdoing it.

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: Contagious?

jokes.text: Why isn't he that funny on the show?
jokes.setup: Hank just did his 'contagious' bit
jokes.punchline: Writer's observation that Hank is funnier off-camera than on
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-commentary. The writer's frustration that Hank's natural comedy doesn't translate to his performance is insightful.

dialogue.speaker: Darlene
dialogue.line: Why isn't he that funny on the show?

jokes.text: I just really appreciate... all the hard work that you do. Maybe Thursday or something, we'll all get together and...
jokes.setup: Larry attempting uncharacteristic appreciation of staff
jokes.punchline: Trailing off mid-sentence, unable to complete fake sentiment
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment showing Larry's inability to be genuinely warm even when trying

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: I just really appreciate... all the hard work that you do. Maybe Thursday or something, we'll all get together and...

jokes.text: The harder they cry, the funnier you are.
jokes.setup: Larry worries about making kids cry
jokes.punchline: Artie's cynical observation that Larry's comedy improves with children's suffering
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy. Artie's brutal honesty about Larry's comedy being enhanced by others' discomfort is perfectly cynical.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: The harder they cry, the funnier you are.

jokes.text: "Tastes great on steak"? What kind of a shit line is that?
jokes.setup: Larry delivers a bad joke about Liz Taylor's perfume
jokes.punchline: Artie's immediate brutal critique of the writing
jokes.explanation: Good behind-the-scenes reality. Artie's immediate disgust with weak material shows the standards gap.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: "Tastes great on steak"? What kind of a shit line is that?

jokes.text: Some friends yesterday insisted that I join them at a smorgasbord. Really? I just had salad. Was it an all-you-can-eat buffet? Yes, it was. Really?
jokes.setup: Hank claims he only had salad at a buffet
jokes.punchline: Larry's repeated 'really' shows he doesn't believe Hank's restraint
jokes.explanation: Good subtle comedy. Larry's skeptical 'really' repetition reveals his doubt about Hank's diet discipline.

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: Some friends yesterday insisted that I join them at a smorgasbord.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: I just had salad.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Was it an all-you-can-eat buffet?

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: Yes, it was.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Really?

jokes.text: I like that guy who goes, "Hey now." Yeah, he's funny when he goes, "Hey now." "Hey now."
jokes.setup: Focus group discussing the show
jokes.punchline: They only know Hank for his catchphrase, reducing him to one thing
jokes.explanation: Good industry satire. Focus group reducing Hank to just his catchphrase reflects how audiences simplify performers.

dialogue.line: I like that guy who goes, "Hey now." Yeah, he's funny when he goes, "Hey now." "Hey now."

jokes.text: There isn't one person in here I haven't seen on America's Most Wanted. I'm telling you, we should be making a phone call right now.
jokes.setup: Larry watching the focus group through one-way glass
jokes.punchline: His paranoid assessment that all the participants are criminals
jokes.explanation: Great Larry paranoia. His assumption that random people are all fugitives is perfectly neurotic and specific.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: There isn't one person in here I haven't seen on America's Most Wanted. I'm telling you, we should be making a phone call right now.

jokes.text: He does have really big nostrils... and they're kind of uneven, but I like that.
jokes.setup: Focus group member talking about liking Larry
jokes.punchline: Backhanded compliment about his uneven, large nostrils
jokes.explanation: Good uncomfortable humor. The specific physical critique disguised as a compliment is awkwardly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Focus group member
dialogue.line: He does have really big nostrils... and they're kind of uneven, but I like that.

jokes.text: I like Larry because he's from Minneapolis, and I'm from Minneapolis. "Hey now." I didn't know he was Canadian.
jokes.setup: One member likes Larry because they're both from Minneapolis
jokes.punchline: Another member thinks Minneapolis is in Canada
jokes.explanation: Decent geography humor. The confusion about Minneapolis being Canadian reflects focus group member intelligence.

dialogue.speaker: Focus group member 1
dialogue.line: I like Larry because he's from Minneapolis, and I'm from Minneapolis. "Hey now."

dialogue.speaker: Focus group member 2
dialogue.line: I didn't know he was Canadian.

jokes.text: Now I know why Baywatch is in the top ten.
jokes.setup: Larry has been listening to focus group feedback
jokes.punchline: He concludes that stupid audiences explain Baywatch's success
jokes.explanation: Good snobbish Larry response. His intellectual superiority complex is on full display with economy.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Now I know why Baywatch is in the top ten.

jokes.text: Twenty people could say they liked me, and I'm telling you... I would still be thinking 17 of them are lying... two of them probably have severe emotional problems... and one of them's probably confusing me with Larry King.
jokes.setup: Larry reflecting on why focus groups don't help his insecurity
jokes.punchline: His specific paranoid breakdown of why any positive feedback would be invalid
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character comedy. Larry's mathematical breakdown of why he can't accept praise is perfectly neurotic and quotable. The Larry King confusion is a great specific detail.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Twenty people could say they liked me, and I'm telling you... I would still be thinking 17 of them are lying... two of them probably have severe emotional problems... and one of them's probably confusing me with Larry King.

jokes.text: The sooner you start liking yourself... you won't care what other people think. Great. Then I'm totally fucked.
jokes.setup: Artie gives Larry self-help advice about self-acceptance
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately realizes this solution is impossible for him
jokes.explanation: Perfect Larry self-awareness. His immediate recognition that self-love is impossible for him is both funny and sad.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: The sooner you start liking yourself... you won't care what other people think.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Great. Then I'm totally fucked.

jokes.text: Thanks. You must be from Minneapolis. No, I'm not. Why? Oh, just a bad guess.
jokes.setup: Fan says he really likes Larry and watches all the time
jokes.punchline: Larry assumes he's from Minneapolis based on focus group feedback
jokes.explanation: Good callback to the focus group geography confusion. Larry's assumption based on flawed data is nicely played.

dialogue.speaker: Steve Himmel
dialogue.line: Thanks.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: You must be from Minneapolis.

dialogue.speaker: Steve Himmel
dialogue.line: No, I'm not.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Why?

dialogue.speaker: Steve Himmel
dialogue.line: Oh, just a bad guess.

jokes.text: Hey, what are you laughing at? Oh, nothing. Are you guys making fun of me? Oh, no, no. Fuck you! Show biz assholes!
jokes.setup: Steve notices Larry and Artie seem to be laughing at something
jokes.punchline: His instant paranoid explosion from fan to enemy
jokes.explanation: Good escalation humor. The fan's instant flip from admiration to hostility when he feels excluded is realistic and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Steve Himmel
dialogue.line: Hey, what are you laughing at?

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Oh, nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Steve Himmel
dialogue.line: Are you guys making fun of me?

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Oh, no, no.

dialogue.speaker: Steve Himmel
dialogue.line: Fuck you! Show biz assholes!

jokes.text: I guess my favorite parts were Martin Mull... the opening monologue, the celebrity products and Hank. Hey now!
jokes.setup: Focus group member listing their favorite parts of the show
jokes.punchline: Another member immediately does Hank's catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Decent callback to the earlier catchphrase obsession. Shows how Hank is reduced to his signature phrase in viewers' minds.

dialogue.speaker: Focus group member
dialogue.line: I guess my favorite parts were Martin Mull... the opening monologue, the celebrity products and Hank. Hey now!

tmdb_overview: Worried about his ratings, Larry hires a focus group to help him tighten up the show.
